title: Probabilistic size-and-shape functional mixed models

reviews.summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.
reviews.strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.
reviews.weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.
reviews.questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.
reviews.limitations: The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper deals with estimation functional data, i.e. time-series represented as functions – under a specific observation model, that aims to separately account for a fixed effect (modeled as a mean function) and other variations (modeled as noise functions), with the added confounding variable being a norm-preserving warping function, that leads to observed data.

The paper proceeds to develop an Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters, under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms, and certain basis functions to represent the functions in a finite dimensional space.

Evaluation is shown both on simulated data and some real functional data examples.

Strengths:
The paper does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models, and their intuition on many example data. I found the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value preserving and norm preserving warps illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The paper tries to motivate the presented mixed effects models by highlighting historical examples based on 2D shape analysis, which provides good context for the proposed developments. 

The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines.

Weaknesses:
Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX. Default parameters are used as given in the R implementation. At the very least some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpmix model and the proposed model should be attempted. Default noise variance parameter in warpmix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher at sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1 etc… Are these comparable in any sense, if not how should this comparison be made more fairly? 

Further, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a model similar to the one used, at least as far as the assumed norm preserving action model. There are papers by the cited authors that use these models for estimating mean functions from a stack of observed functions, mainly by Kurtek, Srivastava and colleagues. Many of these references are also cited in the paper, but some attempt should be made to compare to these approaches. 

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results as shown in figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. There are many ways to take this to the next step of evaluation, to see if some downstream task such as classification shows improvement after the recovery of the needed parameters.

Questions:
I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e. representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated. What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines. I did notice the data-driven functional PCA basis was tried as well, in the appendix, which seemed to perform worse than B-splines.

Additional questions are on evaluation, which are described in the experiments section of the review.

Limitations:
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in section 3.

Rating:
7
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This manuscript introduces a novel Bayesian functional mixed model designed to reliably recover and quantify uncertainty for the 'size-and-shape' of a fixed effect function (µ) in functional data, addressing the challenging problem of disentangling variations along both x and y axes, which are often confounded with additive measurement error. The core innovation lies in leveraging a geometric property of µ that remains invariant to a family of space-time unitary transformations, conceptualized as rotations of the Hilbert space, through a norm-preserving action. This action captures size-and-shape preserving deviations at the object level, while a random linear term and measurement error account for other variations. The paper proceeds to develop a Bayesian approach to estimating the model parameters under certain assumed distributions for the noise terms and basis functions to represent functions in a finite-dimensional space, incorporating informative priors on phase functions for regularization. Evaluation is shown on both simulated data and some real functional data examples, with posterior inference performed via MCMC.

**Strengths:**
The paper presents a highly original approach by introducing the concept of 'size-and-shape' for a fixed effect function in a Bayesian functional mixed model, utilizing a novel norm-preserving action of phase functions. This perspective, along with the interpretation of unitary transformations as data-optimal basis rotations, is a significant and fresh contribution to functional data analysis. The work clearly differentiates itself from existing methods that often rely on value-preserving actions or restrictive error covariance assumptions, with related work adequately cited. The manuscript does a good job of introducing various temporal warping models and their intuition on many example data; the mathematical and visual explanations of the differences between value-preserving and norm-preserving warps are particularly illuminating. The paper is well positioned to advance the field of functional data analysis as more and more applications get impacted by these methods.

The submission is technically sound in its model formulation and Bayesian inference framework using MCMC, with a well-defined model, clear derivations for the marginal likelihood, and a detailed MCMC algorithm provided in the appendices. Claims regarding the model's effectiveness are robustly supported by extensive numerical experiments across diverse simulated and real datasets, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art alternatives like warpMix and BRFC. The paper also has good strategies for actually implementing the infinite dimensional computations using projections onto basis functions such as B-splines. The manuscript is exceptionally well-written and logically organized; Section 2 provides a particularly lucid explanation of phase functions and the distinct properties of the norm-preserving action. Figures are well-designed and contribute significantly to understanding the results, and the comprehensive appendices are invaluable for reproducibility and deeper technical comprehension. The ability to reliably infer the underlying size-and-shape of a population-level function, even in the presence of complex phase variation and measurement noise, is a critical advancement likely to be impactful for researchers and practitioners in various domains.

**Weaknesses:**
While the empirical performance is encouraging, a significant weakness, explicitly acknowledged by the authors, is the current lack of theoretical support for the reliable recovery of the size-and-shape of µ. This indicates that the work is not yet theoretically complete, which raises concerns about its fundamental robustness and generalizability for a novel methodological contribution.

Furthermore, a fundamental mathematical error appears to be present in the derivation of the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio for Prior Model 2 (PM2) in Appendix D.2. The claimed lower triangular structure of the Jacobian (Equations 18 and 19) seems incorrect, implying that its determinant is not simply the product of the diagonal elements. This error, if confirmed, would undermine the reliability of all results obtained using PM2 and invalidate a core component of the proposed Bayesian inference scheme for the more flexible phase model.

Comparisons are limited to a publicly available implementation of the technique known as warpMiX, often using default parameters as given in the R implementation. At the very least, some attempt at matching noise variance assumed in the warpMix model and the proposed model should be attempted, as default noise variance parameter in warpMix is at the level of 10^-3 whereas the noise variances for the proposed model are much higher (e.g., sigma_c^2 = 0.25, sigma^2 = 1). It is unclear if these are comparable, and how a fairer comparison should be made. Beyond warpMix, the paper cites a wealth of literature that uses a similar model, particularly papers by Kurtek, Srivastava, and colleagues for estimating mean functions from observed functions. Some attempt should be made to compare to these established approaches.

Beyond simulation, for the task of recovery of functions (and functional parameters) under an assumed observation model, evaluation should include some real downstream task. Otherwise, we are only faced with visual assessments of quality. For instance, the results in Figure 4 are claimed to be better in the sense that the estimate is ‘as desired and contains sharp features that are representative of the observed data’ (line 351, pg 9). These are at best subjective assessments, and it is unclear if such an estimate is useful in some quantifiable way. Taking the evaluation to the next step, for example, by demonstrating improvement in a downstream task such as classification, would significantly strengthen the paper.

Other limitations include the high computational cost associated with the MCMC algorithm (e.g., 90-111 minutes for n=30 functions), which may hinder its application to very large datasets. The a priori specification of the number of basis functions for fixed and random effects (Bf, Br) is also a practical constraint, with sensitivity analysis indicating that under-specification of Bf can lead to inaccurate results. The description of the "modified Fourier basis" in Section 3.1 is ambiguous and appears to define a non-orthogonal set of functions before Gram-Schmidt, which needs clarification. Moreover, the claim of "unrestricted, nonparametric phase functions" (Section 1) is an overstatement given that PM2 uses a finite-dimensional, piecewise linear approximation. Finally, the specific choice of the error variance structure, `σ^2 ˙γi(tj)`, under the norm-preserving action is a modeling assumption that could benefit from further justification or analysis of its impact.

**Questions:**
1.  **Theoretical Guarantees & "Size-and-Shape" Definition:** Could the authors elaborate on the theoretical roadmap for supporting the empirical superiority of the size-and-shape approach? Specifically, what mathematical properties of the norm-preserving action are expected to make µ more reliably recoverable compared to value-preserving actions? What are the main technical hurdles in developing such guarantees, and are there simplified settings where theoretical results might be more tractable? Please provide a more precise, mathematical definition of 'size-and-shape' as used in this context, especially clarifying how it reconciles with the potential creation of new critical points by Dγ, and how this geometric property relates to other established notions of shape in functional data analysis literature (e.g., elastic shape analysis).
2.  **MCMC Jacobian Derivation (PM2):** Please re-examine the Jacobian derivation for the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio under Prior Model 2 (PM2) in Appendix D.2, specifically Equations (18) and (19). The Jacobian of g(∆(γcan); γcur) appears not to be lower triangular as stated, implying that its determinant is not simply the product of the diagonal elements. This error, if confirmed, would invalidate the MCMC algorithm for PM2 and needs immediate correction.
3.  **Computational Efficiency:** Given the current MCMC runtime, what are the most promising and practical strategies for improving computational efficiency for larger datasets or higher-dimensional functions? Are there specific types of advanced MCMC (e.g., Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, stochastic gradient MCMC) or variational inference methods that would be particularly well-suited, or could simpler approximations for the phase function priors be explored?
4.  **Adaptive Basis Function Selection:** The sensitivity analysis (Appendix H) clearly demonstrates that under-specification of `Bf` can hinder accurate recovery of µ. Have the authors explored or considered incorporating adaptive or automatic methods for selecting `Bf` and `Br`, such as cross-validation, Bayesian nonparametrics, or model selection criteria within the MCMC framework? What are practical guidelines or diagnostic tools for choosing an appropriate Bf, especially when the true underlying µ is unknown, to avoid under-specification?
5.  **Comparison Fairness:** In Example 2 (data generated from `warpMix` model), the proposed Bayesian models often outperform `warpMix`. Could the authors clarify if `warpMix` was run with its default parameter settings or if any effort was made to optimize `warpMix`'s parameters for these specific datasets? A comparison with optimized baselines would strengthen the claim of superiority. Further, some attempt should be made to compare to the approaches by Kurtek, Srivastava, and colleagues, which use similar norm-preserving action models for estimating mean functions.
6.  **"Modified Fourier Basis" Clarity:** Could you provide a more precise definition for the "modified Fourier basis" used for µ in Section 3.1? The elements {√3t, √3(1 − t), √2 cos(2πjt), √2 sin(2πjt); j = 1, 2, . . . ; t ∈ [0, 1]} are not orthogonal to each other (e.g., ∫_0^1 √3t * √3(1-t) dt = 1/2). While Gram-Schmidt is mentioned, the initial set should be clearly defined, perhaps as a basis set *before* orthonormalization, or the specific orthonormal functions should be provided. Also, how does the finite-dimensional, piecewise linear approximation for phase functions in PM2 still qualify as "unrestricted" or "nonparametric" in the context of the infinite-dimensional group Γ?
7.  **Error Variance Structure:** The model assumes that the conditional error variance is scaled by `σ^2 ˙γi(tj)`. Is this specific scaling of the error variance a standard consequence or choice when using the norm-preserving action, or is it a novel modeling assumption? What are the implications of this choice on the posterior inference of µ, particularly if the underlying true error process has a different, unscaled structure?
8.  **Prior Sensitivity:** The paper uses weakly informative priors for a, σ²c, and σ². Could the authors discuss the sensitivity of the results to these specific prior choices, particularly for the fixed effect coefficients 'a' given its very large prior variance? Are there scenarios where more informative priors might be beneficial or necessary?
9.  **BRFC Performance:** In Table 2, the Bayesian model (BRFC) by Cheng et al. (2016) sometimes performs better than some of the proposed models for data generated by warpMix. Could the authors provide further insight into why BRFC might struggle more with data generated from PM1/PM2 compared to warpMix-generated data, especially since BRFC also incorporates phase variation?
10. **Basis Function Approximation Implications:** I would like to know how well the method could be implemented without using basis function approximations – i.e., representing functions simply by their samples. What gets simplified and what gets complicated? What are the practical implications of assuming a specific family of basis functions such as B-Splines?

**Limitations:**
The paper has a reasonable description of the limitations of the model and its assumptions in Section 3, explicitly acknowledging the assumption of common discretization, potential for more complex error structures, computational cost, and the fixed number of basis functions. They also candidly state that "What is lacking is theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress." While commendable for transparency, this indicates a lack of completeness for a rigorous scientific contribution. Crucially, the paper fails to acknowledge the fundamental mathematical error in the derivation of the Jacobian for the PM2 Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio, which is a critical unstated limitation that invalidates a core component of the proposed Bayesian inference scheme for the more flexible phase model. The reliance on a piecewise linear approximation for "unrestricted" phase functions is also a practical limitation not fully highlighted in the context of its theoretical claim.

**Rating:**
7

reviews.summary: The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.
reviews.strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.
reviews.questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?
reviews.limitations: See above

2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense?

Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.

7
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel Bayesian functional mixed model designed to robustly recover a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, particularly addressing the challenges posed by measurement errors and object-level phase variations. The core focus is on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which are hypothesized to remain invariant under a specific class of space-time unitary transformations, conceptualized as rotations of the Hilbert space. The authors propose a norm-preserving action of phase functions, offering a distinct approach from traditional value-preserving methods. The model incorporates informative priors on phase functions and utilizes an MCMC algorithm for posterior inference. The utility and effectiveness of the proposed formulation are demonstrated through extensive numerical experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets, including Berkeley growth data and PQRST complexes, where it empirically shows superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Originality and Novelty:** The paper presents a highly original approach by focusing on the 'size-and-shape' of a fixed effect function within a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The introduction of a norm-preserving action of phase functions, interpreted as data-optimal basis rotations in Hilbert space, is a significant and fresh contribution to functional data analysis. This perspective offers a robust alternative to traditional amplitude-phase separation techniques and is distinct from methods relying on value-preserving actions or restrictive error covariance assumptions.
2.  **Clarity and Presentation:** The manuscript is exceptionally well-written, self-contained, and logically organized. The presentation is precise, and the introduction effectively sets the stage for the problem. Section 2 provides a particularly lucid explanation of phase functions and the distinct properties of the norm-preserving action, aiding intuitive understanding. The model specification, prior distributions, and MCMC algorithm are detailed, with comprehensive appendices enhancing reproducibility and technical comprehension. Figures are well-designed and contribute significantly to understanding the results.
3.  **Technical Soundness and Empirical Validation:** The submission presents a coherent framework and is generally technically sound in its model formulation and Bayesian inference. The claims of improved performance are robustly supported by extensive numerical experiments across diverse simulated and real datasets, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art alternatives like warpMix and BRFC. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions). Sensitivity analyses to hyperparameter misspecification further reinforce the model's reliability.
4.  **Significance:** The work addresses a challenging and important problem in functional data analysis: reliably inferring population-level trends in the presence of complex amplitude and phase variability. The ability to reliably infer the underlying size-and-shape of a population-level function, even with complex phase variation and measurement noise, is a critical advancement. This work is likely to be impactful for researchers and practitioners in various domains, particularly those dealing with biomedical signals or other functional data where phase variability is prominent.

**Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:**

1.  **Significance and Conceptual Foundations of Phase-Preserving Action:**
    *   While the paper introduces a novel phase-preserving (norm-preserving) action, its fundamental significance and applicability to real-world scenarios warrant further clarification. It remains unclear for which practical applications the phase functions are expected to act on the fixed effect µ in a phase-preserving manner, rather than a value-preserving one.
    *   A more precise and mathematical definition of 'size-and-shape' in this context would be beneficial, particularly clarifying how it reconciles with the potential creation of new critical points by the $D_\gamma$ transformation (as mentioned in Section 2). This would strengthen the argument for why this specific definition is superior for reliable recovery compared to other established notions of shape.
    *   The statement in lines 142-147 regarding the "equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense 'larger' than the one under the value-preserving action" requires more rigorous justification. The current explanation is insufficient and could benefit from a formal proof or reference.

2.  **Technical Correctness and Model Specification Details:**
    *   **Critical Issue: Jacobian Derivation for PM2:** A fundamental error appears to exist in the Jacobian calculation for the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio under Prior Model 2 (PM2) in Appendix D.2, specifically Equations (18) and (19). The Jacobian of $g(\Delta(\gamma^{can}); \gamma^{cur})$ is unlikely to be lower triangular as stated, implying that its determinant is not simply the product of the diagonal elements. If confirmed, this error would invalidate the MCMC algorithm for PM2 and undermine the reliability of all results obtained using this more flexible phase model. This critical issue must be re-examined and corrected with utmost urgency.
    *   **Modified Fourier Basis:** The description of the "modified Fourier basis" in Section 3.1 is ambiguous. The initial set of functions $\{ \sqrt{3}t, \sqrt{3}(1-t), \sqrt{2}\cos(2\pi jt), \sqrt{2}\sin(2\pi jt); j=1,2,\dots; t \in [0,1] \}$ is not orthogonal (e.g., $\int_0^1 \sqrt{3}t \cdot \sqrt{3}(1-t) dt = 1/2$). While Gram-Schmidt is mentioned, the initial set should be clearly defined, perhaps as a basis set *before* orthonormalization, or the specific orthonormal functions should be provided for precision.
    *   **"Unrestricted, Nonparametric Phase Functions" Claim:** The claim of considering "unrestricted, nonparametric phase functions" (Section 1) is an overstatement given that Prior Model 2 uses a finite number of discretization points $T_\gamma$, leading to piecewise linear phase functions. The authors should clarify how this finite-dimensional approximation still qualifies as "unrestricted" or "nonparametric" in the context of the infinite-dimensional group $\Gamma$.
    *   **Error Variance Structure:** The model assumes that the conditional error variance is scaled by $\sigma^2 \dot{\gamma}_i(t_j)$. Further justification for this specific scaling choice would be beneficial, along with a discussion of its implications on the posterior inference of µ, especially if the true error process has a different, unscaled structure.

3.  **Empirical Validation and Comparison:**
    *   The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) is somewhat limited. While the proposed model often outperforms warpMix, it is challenging to definitively assess which method is "better" in the absence of ground truth. It would strengthen the claim of superiority if the authors could clarify whether warpMix was run with its default parameter settings or if any effort was made to optimize its parameters for these specific datasets.
    *   In Table 2, the Bayesian model (BRFC) by Cheng et al. (2016) sometimes performs better than some of the proposed models for data generated by warpMix. Further insight into why BRFC might struggle more with data generated from PM1/PM2 compared to warpMix-generated data, especially given that BRFC also incorporates phase variation, would be valuable.

4.  **Limitations and Future Work:**
    *   **Lack of Theoretical Support:** The most significant limitation, explicitly acknowledged by the authors, is the current lack of theoretical support for the reliable recovery of the size-and-shape of µ. While empirical evidence is strong, theoretical guarantees are crucial for a complete understanding, broader acceptance, and generalizability of the method. The authors should elaborate on the main technical hurdles in developing such guarantees and outline their theoretical roadmap.
    *   **Computational Cost:** The MCMC algorithm's high computational cost (e.g., 90-111 minutes for n=30 functions) is a practical limitation that may hinder its application to very large datasets. The authors should outline their planned strategies for improving efficiency, such as exploring advanced MCMC techniques (e.g., Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, parallel tempering), variational inference, or alternative optimization schemes.
    *   **Adaptive Basis Function Selection:** The number of basis functions for both fixed ($B_f$) and random ($B_r$) effects is fixed a priori. The sensitivity analysis indicates that under-specification of $B_f$ can lead to inaccurate results. A principled, data-driven approach (e.g., using information criteria, cross-validation, or Bayesian non-parametric methods) to automatically determine the optimal number of basis functions would be a valuable addition. Practical guidelines or diagnostic tools for choosing an appropriate $B_f$ when the true underlying µ is unknown are also needed.
    *   **Sensitivity to Prior Choices:** While weakly informative priors are used for $a$, $\sigma^2_c$, and $\sigma^2$, a discussion on the sensitivity of the results to these specific prior choices, particularly for the fixed effect coefficients 'a' given its very large prior variance, would be beneficial.

**Minor Comments / Questions:**
1.  Could the authors comment on how using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compares to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log-likelihood and then maximizing it over the parameters?
2.  While the introduction is generally effective, the motivation for introducing phase variability after discussing the challenges of model (1) could be made clearer. The flow could be improved by explicitly stating why model (1) is insufficient and why phase variability is a necessary component earlier.
3.  In Section 2, while the basics of phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided, it is initially unclear why these properties are important or how they contribute to the solution. A brief preamble to Section 2, highlighting the key role these concepts play, would make the flow smoother.
4.  Line 119: Please confirm if "gamma in Gamma" is the intended notation.
5.  Please clarify the notation used for summation in Section 4, specifically $\bar{\gamma} := 1/(nN) \sum^n \sum^N \hat{\gamma}^j_i$. The indices $i=1$ and $j=1$ should be explicitly stated for clarity.

**Decision:**
Accept (with major revisions).

The paper presents a highly original and significant contribution to functional data analysis, offering a novel Bayesian framework for recovering the 'size-and-shape' of a fixed effect function. The clarity of presentation and extensive empirical validation are strong merits. However, the identified critical technical flaw regarding the Jacobian derivation for PM2, along with the acknowledged lack of theoretical support and several conceptual and practical questions, necessitate significant revisions. If these issues, particularly the fundamental technical error, are thoroughly addressed and corrected, the paper will be a valuable addition to the literature.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem of recovering a fixed effect function µ in functional mixed models, where measurement errors and object-level phase variations make the task difficult. It focuses on disentangling the size-and-shape characteristics of µ, which remain invariant under certain transformations. The authors hypothesize that it is feasible to reliably recover the size-and-shape of µ using a Bayesian functional mixed model framework. The formulation is justified by numerical experiments on synthetic and real data.

Strengths:
1. The presentation over all is clear, self-contained, and precise. While this might sound standard in the past, I find these properties rare in papers these days, so I appreciate them even more. 
2. The formulation is carefully backed up by justifications for the choice of components (e.g., basis functions, prior distributions) as well as numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
## Significance:



1. The main proposal of this paper is to integrate phase variations into the model in a phase-preserving manner (equations 3 and 4). Nonetheless, I am struggling to understand what is the significance of such a model. More precisely,
    1. Is this a reasonable model for real applications? Namely, are there some practical applications in which we have reasons to believe the phase functions act on the fixed effect mu in a phase-preserving way?
    2. What are the main conceptual benefits and drawbacks of phase-preserving compared to value-preserving actions? The only answer I seem to find is in lines 142-147, but I do not understand the statement there as I detail in “Clarity”.
2. The comparison of the proposed method with warpMix on real data (Figure 4) seems limited, and this comment relate to the one above on real applications. It is hard to say which method is better when there is no ground-truth - the two methods give different estimates of mu, but it is hard to say which one makes more sense. 

## Clarity:



1. Lines 142-147: Why is “The equivalence class of functions having the same size-and-shape under the norm-preserving action is in an appropriate sense ‘larger’ than the one under the value-preserving action”? 
    1. The explanation following the colon is not sufficient, since the same could be said for value-preserving action: for any g and gamma(t), one can find f(t) := g(gamma^{-1}(t)) such that f(gamma(t)) = g(gamma^{-1}(gamma(t))) = g(t).
    2. The explanation in footnote 1 is a bit hand-wavy and it would be great if rigorous proof could be provided or referred to.

## Novelty:


The idea that the parameterization of the model is not minimal and therefore there is the ambiguity of having an equivalence class of solutions has been there for a long time, as the authors also point out in equation (2). Since (2) is an example in geometric vision which i am a bit more familiar with, I would like to point out more high-level connections here. 


1. A basic idea to resolve the ambiguity is to enforce constraints that favors certain elements in each equivalence class than others. This is more or less what the authors are doing near line 76. Note however that: while two sets of parameters can be equivalent in the sense that if there were no noise then the two sets of parameters explain the observation equally well, in the noisy case the two sets of parameters could handle noise very differently. Therefore, one needs to be smart on which elements in each equivalence class to pick. For example, the work of [A] argues that picking a particular transformation (normalization) in the parameters improves numerical stability of the estimation in the noisy case.
2. Following the same line, two sets of “equivalent” parameters could also be favored differently when the data are corrupted by outliers. An alternative idea to resolve the ambiguity is to estimate the entire equivalent class (rather than finding an element of it), which is for example the path explored in the work of [B].

So far i do not see a strong need for the current paper to exhaust all options, but it would be great if the authors could discuss on how optimal the choice of line 76 is, what might be potential alternatives, etc.

[A] R. I. Hartley, “In defense of the eight-point algorithm,” IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 1997.

[B] Ding, T., Yang, Y., Zhu, Z., Robinson, D. P., Vidal, R., Kneip, L., & Tsakiris, M. C, “Robust homography estimation via dual principal component pursuit”, In the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, 2020.

Questions:
## Minor comments / questions:



1. Could you comment on how does using MCMC to get parameters from the posterior compare to using optimization, e.g., by writing down the log likelihood and then maximize it over the parameters?
2. The introduction was somewhat confusing, as illustrated below. 
    3. The first paragraph motivates the reader with the Berkeley growth data example, saying that a problem for modeling is that the sample size is smaller than the dimension observation, so one must assume prior / constraints in the hypothesis class. Yet the model in equation (1) is rather general and does not assume much prior for recovery.
    4. Now the second paragraph makes the model even more complicated and harder to estimate by introducing phase variability. In particular, why isn’t model (1) sufficient and why does phase variability make sense? 

    To sum up, I think there are multiple problems motivated and highlighted along the way which somehow buries the key issue of phase variability. 

3. The reviewer is grateful that some basics on phase functions and size-and-shape-preserving transformations are provided in section 2. Nonetheless, when I read Section 2 (before going through the rest of the paper) it is unclear why are these properties important - are they going to contribute to the solution? Which key properties allow you to do something that previously was not done? This for example can be said in the preamble of section 2 to make the flow smoother.
4. Line 119: gamma in Gamma?

Limitations:
See above

Rating:
7

Summary:
The paper considers uncertainty quantification for one-dimensional regression tasks, where the observations are noisy. A rather advanced additive model considering invariances under space-time unitary transformations is proposted. Numerical experiments demonstrate the superiority of the approach over other state-of-the-art methods.

Strengths:
- Due to the strong modeling assumptions, on synthethic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as GPs) may fail. 
- The methods seems to be useful to analyze the Berkley growth spurt data set.

Weaknesses:
- It is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience and the considered topic and datasets feel more of a niche. 
- Despite the well-written introduction, the paper was quite difficult to understand due to the advanced math and notations -- after reading it I am still quite unsure why I should really care about functional mixed models and their Bayesian inference.

Questions:
I am slightly puzzled as of why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper - the work may not get the appreciation here it deserves. I spent quite some time on trying to understand the paper, but its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper?   If more general motivations are clarified, I am considering to change my score, but accessibility issues are remaining.

Limitations:
All limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary:**
The paper introduces a novel Bayesian functional mixed model designed to reliably recover and quantify uncertainty for the 'size-and-shape' of a fixed effect function (µ) in one-dimensional regression tasks where observations are noisy. The core innovation lies in leveraging a geometric property of functions, their size-and-shape, which is posited to be invariant to a family of space-time unitary transformations. This approach, conceptualized as data-optimal basis rotations in a functional Hilbert space, aims to disentangle variations along both x and y axes, which are often confounded with additive measurement error. The model incorporates informative priors on phase functions for regularization, and posterior inference is performed via MCMC. Numerical experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets, including the Berkley growth spurt data set and PQRST complexes, demonstrate the model's utility and claim superiority over other state-of-the-art methods like warpMix and BRFC.

**Strengths:**
The paper presents a highly original approach by introducing the concept of 'size-and-shape' for a fixed effect function in a Bayesian functional mixed model, utilizing a novel norm-preserving action of phase functions. This perspective, along with the interpretation of unitary transformations as data-optimal basis rotations, offers a significant and fresh contribution to functional data analysis. Due to these strong modeling assumptions and this innovative geometric framework, on synthetic datasets, the method can recover the true signal even in situations where there is lots of noise and classical methods (such as Gaussian Processes) may fail. The empirical results are compelling, showing consistent improvements over established baselines across various datasets. The model seems to be useful for analyzing real-world functional data, such as the Berkley growth spurt data set and other biomedical signals like PQRST complexes, demonstrating its practical utility in specific domains.

**Weaknesses:**
Despite a well-written introduction that effectively sets the stage, the paper was quite difficult to understand for a general machine learning audience due to the advanced mathematics and specialized notations—even after reading it, the motivation and application to wider machine learning remain unclear. A particularly concerning issue is a fundamental mathematical error identified by an expert reviewer in the Jacobian calculation for the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio under Prior Model 2 (PM2) in Appendix D.2. This error, if confirmed, would invalidate the MCMC algorithm for PM2 and severely compromise the technical soundness and reliability of all results obtained using this more flexible phase model.

Furthermore, it is unclear whether the work will be of interest to the wider machine learning audience, as the considered topic and datasets feel more niche, belonging primarily to the field of functional data analysis. The paper explicitly states that "theoretical support for the same [benefits of the proposed model] is work in progress," indicating a lack of theoretical completeness for a rigorous scientific contribution, particularly concerning the reliable recovery and uncertainty quantification of the proposed size-and-shape of µ. The current computational cost associated with the MCMC algorithm is substantial (e.g., 90-111 minutes for n=30 functions), which could limit its scalability to very large datasets without further optimization. Lastly, the model relies on a priori specification of the number of basis functions for both fixed (Bf) and random (Br) effects, and the sensitivity analysis indicates that under-specification of Bf can lead to inaccurate results.

**Questions:**
Given the identified fundamental mathematical error in the Jacobian derivation for PM2, the authors must re-examine Appendix D.2, specifically Equations (18) and (19). The claimed lower triangular structure of the Jacobian appears to be incorrect, implying that its determinant is not simply the product of the diagonal elements. This error, if confirmed, would invalidate the MCMC algorithm for PM2 and necessitates an immediate and thorough revision of the methodology and results.

I am slightly puzzled as to why NeurIPS was chosen as a venue for this paper. The work may not get the appreciation here it deserves, as its motivations and applications to wider machine learning remain unclear. Perhaps the paper may be better suited towards a statistics journal, such as the ones referenced often in the paper? If more general motivations and broader machine learning implications are clarified, I would reconsider my score, but significant accessibility issues and the newly identified fundamental technical flaws are remaining.

Additionally, the paper claims to consider "unrestricted, nonparametric phase functions" (Section 1). However, Prior Model 2 uses a finite number of discretization points Tγ, leading to piecewise linear phase functions. Could the authors elaborate on how this finite-dimensional approximation still qualifies as "unrestricted" or "nonparametric" in the context of the infinite-dimensional group Γ?

What are the main technical hurdles in developing theoretical guarantees for the reliable recovery and uncertainty quantification of µ's size-and-shape? Also, given the significant computational cost, what are the most promising and practical strategies for improving the efficiency of the MCMC algorithm for larger datasets or higher-dimensional functions?

**Limitations:**
The authors acknowledge several limitations, including the computational cost of MCMC, the assumption of common discretization, and the fixed number of basis functions. They also candidly state that "theoretical support for the same [benefits of the proposed model] is work in progress." While commendable for transparency regarding these points, the paper crucially fails to acknowledge the fundamental mathematical error in the Jacobian derivation for PM2 in Appendix D.2. This unstated limitation is critical, as it invalidates a core component of the proposed Bayesian inference scheme for the more flexible phase model, rendering the method technically unsound in its current form. The reliance on a piecewise linear approximation for "unrestricted" phase functions is also a practical limitation not fully highlighted in the context of its theoretical claim.

**Rating:**
5

reviews.summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.
reviews.strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.
reviews.weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.
reviews.questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
For the problem of the reliable recovery of a fixed effect function $\mu$, this paper focuses on sampling from and summarizing the posterior distribution of a fixed effect function $\mu$ in a functional mixed model with random object-level phase and amplitude components, without a finite-rank covariance assumption on the error process.

----
Post-rebuttal: The rebuttal addressed most of my concerns and I would like to raise my score.

Strengths:
1）	This paper tries to solve a challenging task, recovering the size-and-shape of an unknown function by sampling. 
2）	The theoretical analysis is interesting, e.g., Derivation of Metropolis–Hastings acceptance ratio.
3）	The authors also give the convergence analysis by numerical experiments.

Weaknesses:
1)	It seems that we should give different prior models for different functions.
2)	The parameter sensitivity of $\theta$ and the running time of the proposed method should be given.

Questions:
1)	Neural networks can also recover functions by sampling. What are the advantages of the proposed method compared to neural networks?
2)	How can we recover arbitrary by a fixed model in Eq.(3)?

5

reviews.summary: In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.
reviews.strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.
reviews.weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.
reviews.questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?
reviews.limitations: The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator). 
 
- A couple of linear combinations of basis functions are assumed. The rank of every subspace is Bf and Br. This type of formulation has been proposed in previous approaches, following an LDA style. The rank of every subspace is known a prior. 
 
- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario. 
 
Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. 
 
The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

Rating:
5
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This manuscript proposes a novel Bayesian functional mixed model designed to reliably recover and quantify uncertainty for the 'size-and-shape' of a fixed effect function (µ) in noisily observed functional data, accounting for population- and object-level variability. The core innovation lies in leveraging a geometric property of functions—their size-and-shape—which is invariant to a family of space-time unitary transformations (norm-preserving action Dγ). This action is conceptualized as data-optimal rotations of the Hilbert space basis, aiming to disentangle variations along both x and y axes from additive measurement error. The model incorporates random object-level unitary transformations and linear terms, regularized by informative priors on phase functions. While synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with existing methods demonstrate how a posterior mean under the proposed model captures main properties of the function, the paper currently suffers from several critical technical and methodological issues that need to be addressed.

**Strengths:**
*   **Original and Novel Approach:** The paper presents a highly original approach by introducing the concept of 'size-and-shape' for a fixed effect function within a Bayesian functional mixed model, utilizing a novel norm-preserving action of phase functions. This perspective, along with the interpretation of unitary transformations as data-optimal basis rotations, is a significant and fresh contribution to functional data analysis, clearly differentiating itself from existing methods. The proposed Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is a key innovation.
*   **Technical Soundness (Conceptual):** The model is conceptually well-defined, and the overall Bayesian inference framework using MCMC is coherently presented. The authors provide clear derivations for the marginal likelihood and a detailed MCMC algorithm in the appendices, which are invaluable for reproducibility and deeper technical comprehension.
*   **Empirical Performance:** Claims regarding the model's effectiveness are robustly supported by extensive numerical experiments across diverse simulated and real datasets. The proposed model consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art alternatives like warpMix and BRFC, demonstrating its utility in estimating µ even in the presence of complex phase variation and measurement noise.
*   **Clarity and Organization:** The manuscript is generally well-written and logically organized. The introduction effectively sets the stage, and Section 2 provides a lucid explanation of phase functions and the distinct properties of the norm-preserving action. The model specification is clear, aided by a helpful analogy to landmark shape analysis, and most explanations and discussions are provided, making it easy to follow up to certain formulation points. Figures are well-designed and contribute significantly to understanding the results.
*   **High Significance:** The results are highly significant for the field of functional data analysis. The ability to reliably infer the underlying size-and-shape of a population-level function is a critical advancement. This work addresses a challenging identifiability problem and introduces a unique geometric and statistical framework, with potential broader implications for machine learning, such as incorporating geometric constraints in neural networks.

**Weaknesses:**
*   **Critical Technical Flaw in MCMC Derivation:** A fundamental and critical error appears to be present in the derivation of the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio for Prior Model 2 (PM2) in Appendix D.2. The claimed lower triangular structure of the Jacobian (Equations 18 and 19) is incorrect, which implies that its determinant is not simply the product of the diagonal elements. This error, if confirmed, would invalidate the MCMC algorithm for PM2, severely compromising the reliability of all results obtained using this more flexible phase model. This is a major concern that needs immediate attention.
*   **Lack of Theoretical Support:** The authors candidly acknowledge that theoretical support for the empirically demonstrated benefits of the size-and-shape approach is currently lacking and "work in progress." While empirical performance is strong, the absence of theoretical guarantees for this novel concept raises concerns about its fundamental robustness, generalizability, and complete understanding.
*   **Notation and Clarity:** The notation could be significantly improved. For example, the use of vectors, matrices, and scalars could be differentiated more clearly, and including dimensionality in some cases would aid readers. Some symbols, such as the transpose operator, are inconsistent throughout the text. Additionally, the description of the "modified Fourier basis" in Section 3.1 is ambiguous and appears to define a non-orthogonal set of functions before Gram-Schmidt, which should be clarified for precision. The claim of "unrestricted, nonparametric phase functions" (Section 1) is also an overstatement given that PM2 uses a finite-dimensional, piecewise linear approximation.
*   **A Priori Basis Function Specification:** The model assumes linear combinations of basis functions, where the rank of every subspace (Bf and Br) is known a priori. This type of formulation, following an LDA style, has been proposed previously. The sensitivity analysis indicates that under-specification of Bf can lead to inaccurate results, suggesting a need for more adaptive or data-driven selection mechanisms.
*   **High Computational Cost:** A primary practical limitation, explicitly acknowledged, is the high computational cost associated with the MCMC algorithm (e.g., 90-111 minutes for n=30 functions). This may hinder its application to very large datasets or higher-dimensional functions without further optimization.
*   **Lack of Practical Motivation:** The authors could include a more explicit practical motivation for the study, detailing where their approach could be applied and why it is important in real-world scenarios.
*   **Error Variance Structure:** The specific choice of the error variance structure, `σ^2 ˙γi(tj)`, under the norm-preserving action is a modeling assumption that could benefit from further justification or analysis of its impact.

**Questions:**
*   **Regarding the Critical Technical Flaw:** Please re-examine the Jacobian derivation for the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio under Prior Model 2 (PM2) in Appendix D.2, specifically Equations (18) and (19). If the Jacobian is not lower triangular as stated, this error would invalidate the MCMC algorithm for PM2 and must be corrected.
*   **Theoretical Guarantees:** Could the authors expand on the theoretical roadmap for supporting the empirical superiority of the size-and-shape approach? What are the main technical hurdles in developing such guarantees, and are there specific simplified settings or assumptions under which theoretical results might be more tractable? How might the absence of such theoretical support impact the generalizability or robustness of the empirical findings?
*   **Basis Function Selection:** How is the rank of both subspaces (Bf and Br) fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically, perhaps using information criteria, cross-validation, or Bayesian non-parametric methods? This seems to be a key factor, but the authors do not provide sufficient discussion in this line. Could the authors provide practical guidelines or diagnostic tools for choosing an appropriate Bf, especially when the true underlying µ is unknown, to avoid under-specification?
*   **Specific Basis Choice:** The authors propose to use a B-spline basis. Why this specific type of function, given the many piecewise polynomials available in literature? Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? Furthermore, could you provide a more precise definition for the "modified Fourier basis" used for µ in Section 3.1, clarifying its initial orthogonality properties? How does the choice of basis (Fourier vs. B-spline) interact with the "rotation" induced by the norm-preserving action, and what is its impact on the interpretability of the recovered µ's size-and-shape?
*   **Computational Efficiency:** Given the significant computational cost of the MCMC algorithm, what are the most promising and practical strategies for improving efficiency for larger datasets or higher-dimensional functions? Are there specific types of advanced MCMC (e.g., Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, stochastic gradient MCMC) or variational inference methods that would be particularly well-suited?
*   **Data Scale Inconsistency:** In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic data, n=100,000. Why does this difference need to be so big? If the goal is to address problems in scenarios with limited data, this should also be considered in the synthetic scenario.
*   **Comparisons and Robustness:** While the comparison with warpMix is appreciated, the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. Additionally, what about computational cost trade-off (accuracy vs. cost) for both methods? Were baseline methods like warpMix run with their default parameter settings, or was an effort made to optimize their parameters for these specific datasets? What about failure cases of the proposed method? Could the authors provide further insight into why BRFC might struggle more with data generated from PM1/PM2 compared to warpMix-generated data, especially since BRFC also incorporates phase variation?
*   **Prior Sensitivity:** The paper uses weakly informative priors for a, σ²c, and σ². Could the authors discuss the sensitivity of the results to these specific prior choices, particularly for the fixed effect coefficients 'a' given its very large prior variance? Are there scenarios where more informative priors might be beneficial or necessary?
*   **Definition of 'Size-and-Shape':** In Section 2, the paper states that the norm-preserving action 'may create new critical points, but in a way such that its size-and-shape... is preserved.' This statement appears to contradict a common intuitive understanding of 'shape' as preserving topological features like critical points. Could the authors provide a more precise, mathematical definition of 'size-and-shape' as used in this context, and clarify how it reconciles with the potential creation of new critical points by Dγ? How does this geometric property relate to other established notions of shape in functional data analysis literature (e.g., elastic shape analysis in SRVF framework, or landmark-based shape analysis), particularly in terms of identifiability and robustness to noise?
*   **Figure Citation and Notation:** Figure 1 (b) and (c) are not cited in the introduction. Please clarify the notation used for summation in Section 4, specifically ¯γ := 1/(nN ) ∑^n ∑^N ŷγ^j_i, by explicitly stating the indices i=1 and j=1 for clarity.

**Limitations:**
The authors acknowledge several limitations, including the assumption of common discretization, potential for more complex error structures, computational cost, and the current reliance on fixed basis functions. They also candidly state that "theoretical support for the same, and this is work in progress," which indicates a lack of completeness for a rigorous scientific contribution. However, the authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper, particularly failing to acknowledge the fundamental mathematical error in the derivation of the Jacobian for the PM2 Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio, which is a crucial unstated limitation. The reliance on a piecewise linear approximation for "unrestricted" phase functions is also a practical limitation not fully highlighted in the context of its theoretical claim. While the sensitivity to basis choice is explored, a deeper analysis of how the choice of basis might interact with the "rotation" induced by the norm-preserving action, and its impact on the interpretability of the recovered µ's size-and-shape, would be beneficial. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

**Rating:**
5

**Decision:**
This paper presents a novel and potentially significant contribution to functional data analysis by introducing a Bayesian functional mixed model that leverages a norm-preserving action to infer the 'size-and-shape' of a fixed effect function. The empirical results are compelling, demonstrating superior performance over existing state-of-the-art methods. However, the manuscript currently suffers from several critical technical and methodological issues that necessitate major revisions. Most notably, the identified fundamental error in the Jacobian calculation for the MCMC algorithm under Prior Model 2 is a severe flaw that undermines the reliability of a core component of the proposed method. Additionally, the lack of theoretical support for the size-and-shape concept, clarity issues in notation and basis definitions, and the need for more robust basis function selection methods are significant concerns. While the potential impact of this work is high, these issues must be thoroughly addressed and corrected for the paper to be considered technically sound and fully acceptable. Therefore, I recommend a **Weak Accept** with the understanding that **major revisions** are required to resolve the identified critical flaws and thoroughly address the numerous questions and limitations.

Summary:
In this paper is proposed a mixed model in a functional Hilbert space for a size-and-shape (a type of geometric property) of a square-integrable fixed effect. To this end, the authors consider an isometric action of the infinite-dimensional group of phase functions. Synthetic and real experiments and comparisons with respect to another method in literature are reported, demonstrating how a posterior mean, and under the proposed model, captures the main properties of the function.

Strengths:
+ A Bayesian functional mixed effects model with an unrestricted form of phase variation is presented. 
 
+ A novel perspective of inferring a size-and-shape function, a type of geometric constraint. 
 
+ In general, the paper is concise and direct. Most explanations and discussions are provided. It is easy to follow, up to some points in formulation.

Weaknesses:
- I think the notation could be improved, representing, for example, the use of vectors, matrices and scalars differently. In some cases, including dimensionality may help readers. Moreover, some symbols are different along text (see the transpose operator).

- The authors could include a practical motivation of the study, where they could apply their approach and why it is important.

Questions:
How is the rank of both subspaces fixed? By hand? How sensitive is the solution with respect to these parameters? Could Bf and Br be inferred automatically? This seems to be a key factor in the model, but the authors do not provide any discussion in this line. 
 
The authors propose to use a B-spline basis, so a pre-defined one. Why this type of function? Note that we can find many piecewise polynomials in literature. 
 
Could both basis functions be learned jointly with weight coefficients? 
 
In real data, n=93, but when the authors use synthetic one, n=100,000. I cannot understand why the difference needs to be so big. In my opinion, if the goal is to sort out the problem in scenarios where the amount of data is limited, this should be also considered in the synthetic scenario.

The method consistently outperforms warpMix on synthetic data. While I like this comparison, I think the authors should consider more datasets for this evaluation. In addition to that, what about computational cost? I would like to see the trade-off accuracy vs. cost for both methods. 

What about failure cases?

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze properly the limitations of the paper. In any case, I do not think the work in this paper can produce a negative social impact.

title: Chain of Preference Optimization: Improving Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Chain-of-Preference Optimization (CPO) to improve reasoning in LLMs. CPO utilizes preference data generated during ToT to fine-tune models. This approach improves reasoning without increasing inference complexity.
reviews.strengths:
1. The idea of CPO is interesting.
2. CPO achieves enhanced performance without increasing inference complexity.
3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. While CPO is effective across several tasks, its performance on more complex reasoning benchmarks, such as the MATH benchmark, remains unexplored.
2. Fine-tuning with reasoning paths generated by ToT may reduce the diversity of generated paths. A systematic investigation into how the generated reasoning paths change before and after fine-tuning is needed.
3. The effectiveness of CPO is heavily dependent on the quality of reasoning paths generated by ToT. If ToT produces suboptimal paths, the preference data might not be as beneficial.
4. The paper does not adequately address the applicability of CPO to more advanced models, such as LLaMA3.
5. While CPO enhances performance, the paper lacks a deep analysis of the interpretability of the optimized reasoning paths. Understanding why certain paths are preferred could provide additional insights and improve trust in the model’s decisions.
reviews.limitations: Yes
